narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there amid the challenges many face. Your application included your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, an LSAT score of 30, rankings in the 1st decile for Year 1 and 2nd decile for Year 3 at your school, a middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 90% chance you would fail the bar—far higher than the typical student's risk—which led to the tough decision to reject your application right now. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, well below the average of around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance compared to peers who typically pass. Your Year 1 decile in the bottom ranks also contributed significantly in the same direction, standing out as lower than most successful students' early records. Even your undergraduate GPA, at 2.50 versus the usual 3.21, added to the model's concern about consistent academic strength. Your middle family income and LSAT score played smaller roles but still nudged the prediction toward higher risk, unlike the stronger averages seen in bar passers, while your full-time status had minimal sway since most students are in that category. Overall, these elements made your profile look riskier than the typical admitted student, whose grades and test scores align more closely with bar success stories. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes patterns that predict passing on the first try to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience as a Black woman in this field. Reapply after addressing these, perhaps targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your unique background, and consider bar prep resources early. You've got this—keep pushing, and we're rooting for your success.
